fasting is from the break of dawn until the sun set and that those things which break a person's fast are deliberate eating and drinking and sexual intercourse these are things that are agreed upon the most scholars of islam the difference of opinion comes in the the very fine details and that is that we have to understand something as a matter of fact scholars of islam have written books with regards to why the scholars of islam differ on certain rulings so for example sheikh islam even allah he wrote a book called he has a treat he's very short book which he has entitled lifting any blame from the scholars of islam and what he set out to articulate in this book is that why would a scholar of islam purposely adopt a ruling or adopt an opinion that directly contradicts a verse of the quran or sunnah of the prophet sallallahu alaihi right this is this is what that book was about very good book and he mentioned six six different things that different different factors six factors that may contribute to what to us in hindsight would seem to be that this scholar has directly contradicted a clear narration of the prophet sallallahu alaihi wasallam meaning the prophet sallallahu alaihi wasallam says yes and this scholar says no why is that why does that happen it does happen it is anomalous but why does it happen well he mentions six excuses for that the first

excuse is the scholar may just not know that narration and that's very plausible not only is that plausible but it is highly possible right it is highly possible and he begins that book with mentioning the instances in which abu bakr al-sadiq who was the prophet sallallahu alaihi wasallam's friend and companion before islam i mean he was his friend and his close confidant before he even became a prophet i mean if anybody knows the prophet muhammad sallallahu alaihi even better than his wives because he is the sole companion who spent the most time with the prophet muhammad sallallahu alaihi and from this the scholars of islam such as mentioned that abu bakr al-sadiq is the most knowledgeable companion from amongst all of the companions but there are instances albeit few in which he was unaware of a narration and he was informed of this narration by others of the companions who have much less time spent with the prophet sallallahu alaihi wasallam he mentions instances in which was unaware of the narration and was told by other companions who had become muslim way later such as abu musa al-ashadi uthman ibn afan he mentions 40 authentic instances throughout his khilafah and life in which ali was unaware of the narration the statement of the prophet salallahu why is that were they slacking off were they not trying to learn no it's just that as imam hashef so eloquently said whoever claims that he knows all of

the sunnah has made a very incorrect statement he says nobody knows all of the sunnah from amongst mankind except for the prophet muhammad so it's just not possible the body of information is too vast and for us to say that yes this scholar knows every single narration every single they may even know the narration they just don't they they haven't noticed the that it is an evidence for that particular thing that they are mentioning they may not know the narration they may not grade the narration authentic they may not understand the narration as others have understood it they may have seen that other things contradict that narration right so there are reasons our issue is not to worship scholars we don't worship scholars we don't holify them we don't view them as infallible saints no that's not correct a scholar's job is merely to deliver the information and to explain it to us that's it a scholar does not make up new rules new laws he doesn't have any special powers a scholar's position is to articulate and pass down what the prophet salallahu alaihi wasallam brought to us and a scholar's position is to explain what we are unaware of or unsure of that's it and a scholar can make a mistake it's not the norm it's improbable if the scholar is worth his salt but it is something that happens with everyone because we are human beings yes the entirety of the sunnah

is entirely preserved and known to all of the ummah at once as a whole yes but to one individual no the knowledge is too vast and so in conclusion when we discuss the prayer of the prophet salallahu said there's going to be a lot of oh i didn't hear it that way i heard it a different way i learned it a different way ibn clayem doesn't even get into that he is now going to just transmit to us what was authentically reported and anything that was not authentically reported he doesn't even mention right and so we are going to learn that and this is why we chose this book so that is enough for our session today insha allah or tonight insha allah next week we will continue on and we will begin to discuss in detail the way that the prophet muhammad sallallahu alaihi wasallam in front of his lord subhanahu wa'ta'ala we ask allah subhanahu wa'ta'ala that he bless us beneficial knowledge that he teach us of his revelations what we are ignorant of that he remind us what we have been made to forget and that he benefit us with understanding and knowledge and action from this teaching muhammad.

summary: This lecture covers detailed Islamic jurisprudence on purification (taharah), specifically focusing on the invalidators of wudu (ablution), the rulings surrounding tayammum (dry ablation), and the proper method of ghusl (full body ritual washing). The speaker discusses common misconceptions about these practices, including what breaks wudu (such as touching private parts, eating camel meat, and deep sleep), the conditions under which ghusl is obligatory (sexual impurity, menstruation, and postpartum bleeding), and clarifies that a regular shower only counts as ghusl if done with the explicit intention of worship. The lecture emphasizes that ghusl performed for ritual purification replaces the need for wudu, and provides the sunnah method of ghusl as practiced by the Prophet Muhammad, while also explaining the minimum requirements for validity.
suggested_title: Purification in Islam: Wudu, Tayammum, and Ghusl - Invalidators and Proper Methods
